_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Adams County, MS. Adams County was formed in 1799 from the Natchez District and is one of the oldest counties in Mississippi. Natchez, the county seat, was the center of the antebellum cotton aristocracy and one of the wealthiest communities in the pre-Civil War South. Records reflect layered sovereignty: French (1716-1763), British (1763-1779), Spanish (1779-1798), and American (1798-present). The Forks of the Road slave market operated in Natchez, making Adams County records critical for documenting the domestic slave trade.

current_courthouse.location: Natchez, MS — 128 South Wall Street (extensively remodeled 1880s and later, but the 1819 structure persists as one of the oldest courthouses in continuous use in Mississippi)

previous_courthouses.location: Fort Rosalie, Natchez — French colonial administration conducted legal business at the fort and in colonial administrative buildings
previous_courthouses.fate: French colonial records were removed to Mobile and New Orleans during the transition to British control. Some survive in French colonial archives (Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer, Aix-en-Provence) and in Louisiana State archives.

previous_courthouses.location: Natchez — British administrative offices in the town
previous_courthouses.fate: British-era records were partially removed when Spain seized the Natchez District in 1779. Some survive in British colonial records (Public Record Office, London) and in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.

Spanish colonial records are among the most genealogically valuable for Adams County. Governor Gayoso de Lemos maintained systematic records including land grants, censuses, and legal proceedings. Many survive in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History and in Seville (Archivo General de Indias). The Spanish period records include detailed censuses of the Natchez District in 1784, 1787, 1792, and 1794.

Records survive from the territorial period. Adams County was organized in 1799 as one of the first two counties in the Mississippi Territory (with Pickering County). Territorial court records are held at the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.

record_losses.description: Adams County is one of the best-preserved counties in Mississippi for genealogical records. The courthouse survived the Civil War — Union forces occupied Natchez in July 1863 after the fall of Vicksburg, but the city surrendered without significant destruction. No major courthouse fire has occurred. The primary record challenge is not loss but the layered sovereignty: French, British, Spanish, and American records are held in different repositories and require different languages and paleographic skills to read.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Adams County after 1799, the American-era records almost certainly survive in Natchez. The deeper question is whether {pronoun_possessive} family predates American sovereignty — and if so, French, British, or Spanish colonial records in distant archives may hold the earliest documentation.
record_losses.source: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.

records_destroyed: some loose papers scattered during occupation
record_losses.description: Union forces under General Ulysses S. Grant occupied Natchez on July 13, 1863, days after the fall of Vicksburg. Natchez surrendered without a fight — the city's planter elite calculated that resistance would bring destruction. The courthouse records were not systematically damaged. Some Confederate-era records were removed or scattered, and Chancery Court operations were disrupted during occupation, but the bound volumes survived intact.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The Union occupation of Natchez disrupted daily record-keeping but did not destroy the courthouse or its records. If {ancestor_name} conducted legal business between 1863 and 1865, there may be gaps in court minutes, but deeds and wills recorded before and after the occupation survive.
record_losses.source: Davis, Ronald L. F. Good and Faithful Labor: From Slavery to Sharecropping in the Natchez District, 1860-1890. Westport, CT: Greenwood Press, 1982.

deeds.completeness: Excellent — continuous series from American territorial period. Spanish-era land grants survive at MDAH and provide earlier documentation for some properties.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps in the American period. Pre-1798 deeds require searching colonial archives.
deeds.repository: Adams County Chancery Clerk, Natchez; Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com MS land records; MDAH digital collections

wills_estates.completeness: Very good — wills and estate inventories survive from early in the county's history. Estate inventories for wealthy planters are extensive and may include detailed lists of enslaved people by name.
wills_estates.gaps: Some loose estate papers from the earliest decades are fragmentary. Bound will books are intact.
wills_estates.repository: Adams County Chancery Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; limited MDAH digital collections

marriages.completeness: Very good — marriage bonds and licenses survive from the territorial period. Mississippi required marriage licenses from statehood (1817).
marriages.gaps: Pre-1799 marriages must be reconstructed from church records (Catholic parish for Spanish period) or colonial administrative records.
marriages.repository: Adams County Circuit Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

court_minutes.completeness: Good — court minutes survive from the territorial period through the present. Chancery Court records are particularly rich.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor disruption during Civil War occupation (1863-1865).
court_minutes.repository: Adams County Circuit Clerk and Chancery Clerk; Mississippi Department of Archives and History

vital_records.completeness: Mississippi statewide registration began 1912. Compliance was incomplete until the 1920s. Earlier vital events must be reconstructed from church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices.
vital_records.repository: Mississippi State Department of Health, Vital Records; MDAH for delayed certificates

church_records.parish: St. Mary Basilica (Catholic — continuous from Spanish period), First Presbyterian Church of Natchez (1817), various Baptist and Methodist congregations
church_records.completeness: Catholic parish records from the Spanish period are among the most important genealogical sources for early Adams County. They include baptisms, marriages, and burials for white, free Black, and enslaved populations. Protestant church records begin after American sovereignty.
church_records.repository: Diocese of Natchez-Jackson archives; individual congregations; MDAH
church_records.online_access: Some Catholic records available through Diocese archives; limited FamilySearch digitization

substitute_sources.description: The Spanish governors of the Natchez District conducted detailed censuses in 1784, 1787, 1792, and 1794. These enumerate free white households, free people of color, and enslaved people — often with more detail than early American census schedules. Spanish land grants document property transfers and settlement patterns. Governor Manuel Gayoso de Lemos maintained particularly thorough records.
substitute_sources.repository: Mississippi Department of Archives and History, Jackson; Archivo General de Indias, Seville; published abstracts and translations
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For families in the Natchez District before 1798, Spanish records may be the only detailed documentation. The censuses provide household composition, ages, and sometimes occupations — data the first American censuses (1800, 1810) do not provide at individual level.
substitute_sources.source: Holmes, Jack D. L. Gayoso: The Life of a Spanish Governor in the Mississippi Valley, 1789-1799. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1965.

substitute_sources.source_type: Natchez Trace travelers' accounts and records
substitute_sources.description: The Natchez Trace connected Natchez to Nashville and was a major overland route from the late 18th through early 19th centuries. Travelers' accounts, inn registers, and military records along the Trace document names and movements of people passing through Adams County.
substitute_sources.repository: Tennessee State Library and Archives; Mississippi Department of Archives and History; Natchez Trace Parkway archives (National Park Service)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Useful for ancestors who migrated through the Natchez District via the Trace. Inn registers and military muster rolls document travelers. The Trace was the primary overland return route for flatboatmen who floated goods down the Mississippi to Natchez or New Orleans.
substitute_sources.source: Daniels, Jonathan. The Devil's Backbone: The Story of the Natchez Trace. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1962.

substitute_sources.source_type: Federal census schedules and slave schedules
substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1800-1940. Slave schedules (1850-1860) are critical for Adams County, which had one of the highest enslaved populations per capita in the South. Agricultural schedules document the cotton economy.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Slave schedules for Adams County in 1850 and 1860 list slaveholders and the age, sex, and color of enslaved people. Estate inventories and wills in the courthouse sometimes name enslaved people individually. Cross-referencing slave schedules with estate records is essential for documenting enslaved families in Adams County.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Adams County, MS.

substitute_sources.description: The Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands operated in the Natchez District from 1865 to 1872. Records include labor contracts between former slaveholders and freedpeople, complaints, school reports, and marriage registrations that attempted to formalize unions formed during slavery.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives (RG 105); FamilySearch digitized Freedmen's Bureau records; Freedmen and Southern Society Project, University of Maryland
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for tracing formerly enslaved families in Adams County. Labor contracts name individuals, their former owners, and sometimes family relationships. Marriage registrations record unions that had existed during slavery but had no legal standing — often naming the year the couple began living together and their children.
substitute_sources.source: Freedmen and Southern Society Project. Freedom: A Documentary History of Emancipation, 1861-1867. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, multiple volumes.

narrative_hooks: Adams County's records stretch back to the Spanish colonial period — and if {ancestor_name}'s family was in the Natchez District before 1798, the Spanish censuses of 1784, 1787, 1792, and 1794 may hold {pronoun_possessive} name in a governor's hand, written in a language {pronoun_subject} likely did not speak.

The courthouse in Natchez has stood since 1819 — one of the oldest in continuous use in Mississippi. If {ancestor_name} bought land, made a will, or stood before a judge in Adams County, the record is almost certainly still in that building.

Adams County's estate inventories are devastating documents — they list {ancestor_name}'s property in brutal detail, and if that property included human beings, the inventory names them, gives their ages, and assigns each a dollar value. The record survives because it was legally required. The people it catalogued survived in spite of it.

The Natchez Trace brought {ancestor_name} overland from Tennessee through Choctaw country to the bluff above the river — or carried word of {pronoun_possessive} arrival ahead of {pronoun_object}. The Trace was not a road but a beaten path through wilderness, and the inn registers along the way may document {pronoun_possessive} passage.

The Freedmen's Bureau in Natchez recorded what slavery had refused to: the names of {ancestor_name}'s family members, the date they had begun living together, the children born without legal record. The Bureau marriage registration was not the beginning of the family — it was the first time the law admitted the family existed.
source_references: Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Guide to Official Records in the Mississippi Department of Archives and History. Jackson, 1994.
Holmes, Jack D. L. Gayoso: The Life of a Spanish Governor in the Mississippi Valley, 1789-1799. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 1965.
Davis, Ronald L. F. Good and Faithful Labor: From Slavery to Sharecropping in the Natchez District, 1860-1890. Westport, CT: Greenwood Press, 1982.
Daniels, Jonathan. The Devil's Backbone: The Story of the Natchez Trace. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1962.
Freedmen and Southern Society Project. Freedom: A Documentary History of Emancipation, 1861-1867. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, multiple volumes.
